sources.notes: Maintain the minimum removal cost as selected costs are set to zero over time.
Official editorial defines a recursive function around interval maxima, saves the recursion as a tree, and updates affected subtrees when costs become zero.

annotation.summary: Given arrays of values and removal costs, output the minimum total removal cost initially and after each query that sets one cost to zero.
annotation.constraints: n is large, so recomputing the optimal removal process after every query is impossible.
annotation.core_idea: The optimal removal structure is recursive around maximum elements of an interval: maxima split the interval and at least one outside/maximum cost controls each segment. This recursion forms a tree of responsibility for which elements pay which removal cost. A query that sets c_i to zero only affects the subtree where i belongs as a relevant maximum/killer, so DFS/DSU-style skipping updates each node at most once.
annotation.complexity: O(n log n) or near-linear after building the max-recursion tree, plus amortized O(n) over all zeroing updates.

solution_variants.name: max-decomposition-update-tree
solution_variants.summary: Build the recursive maximum-decomposition tree of removal costs and process zero-cost queries by traversing newly affected subtrees once.
solution_variants.complexity: O(n log n) preprocessing plus amortized O(n) query work

tags.evidence: The optimal structure is the recursive decomposition of intervals by maximum elements, equivalent to a Cartesian-tree-like hierarchy.
tags.description: Use min/max Cartesian tree or equivalent interval-extremum recursion to represent array structure.
tags.created_reason: This captures a common array decomposition pattern that generic divide-and-conquer does not identify.

tags.evidence: Queries update the stored recursive tree by DFS over affected subtrees.
tags.description: Depth-first traversal over an explicit tree to propagate or collect state.
tags.created_reason: Tree DFS is common enough to be searchable separately from generic dfs/trees.

tags.evidence: Affected nodes can be skipped after they are zeroed/processed, giving amortized query time.
tags.description: Disjoint-set or next-pointer structure used to skip already processed positions/nodes.
tags.created_reason: The DSU tag is too broad for amortized skip-pointer updates.

tags.evidence: Each affected tree node is visited only the first time its responsible cost becomes zero.
tags.description: Design updates so each element/node is deleted, activated, or processed at most once.
tags.created_reason: This amortization pattern recurs across data-structure update problems.
